data.title: Commercial Real Estate: It's More Diverse Than You Think
data.description:
By Scott Greenfield and Brian Resnick, CFA

The opportunity set in this wide-ranging market extends well beyond office properties.

Investors often view commercial real estate (CRE) through the narrow lens of the office sector. We think this office-only focus understates how broad the asset class is and its potential. Offices may face well-documented headwinds, but many other CRE segments appear more resilient.

CRE spans a diverse range of asset types, including multifamily housing, industrial and logistics, hospitality, and niche sectors investors can access through both public and private markets. At a foundational level, we think CRE credit offers a distinct profile within fixed income. Loans are secured by buildings or properties that generate income and have their own value.

Unlike intellectual property or other intangible assets, we think real estate is less susceptible to being displaced by technology. Advances in artificial intelligence, for example, aren’t likely to stop people from living in apartment buildings or using warehouses for storage. In our view, that’s why much of this tangible collateral keeps its value - even in volatile markets.

CRE Deals Often Have Sizable Buffers for Lenders

The structural framework underpinning these investments is equally important. Real estate deals typically include substantial borrower equity that’s subordinate to the debt used to finance them. This equity creates a sizable buffer for credit investors in case the asset’s value declines.

These buffers are key, because, if property values decline enough, lenders take losses at a certain point. In the example below, the property’s value would have to decline by more than 30% before lenders would face losses (Display). This buttress enables credit investors to make generating income and having downside mitigation a priority instead of relying on the property value rising.

What’s more, we think commercial real estate debt’s combination of seniority, contractual income streams and physical collateral can foster durability across economic cycles. For investors, that resilience can shift the emphasis away from trying to time the real estate market. In our view, it also helps explain the historical staying power of CRE credit, even in uncertain environments.

A Transition, Not Decline, for Commercial Real Estate

We think the CRE landscape is in a period of transition - not broad-based contraction. Capital markets have recovered from their 2025 lows, thanks in part to increased participation from private credit providers, insurance investors and structured-products markets.

And while many office properties face challenges from changing work habits and what employers and employees want from a modern office, that doesn’t add up to broad-based decline. Asset quality and location matter. What’s more, developers are increasingly adapting unwanted offices for other purposes.

In sectors beyond office space, the CRE picture is brighter. Demand for industrial property remains stable, while high-quality hotels and resorts continue to outperform. Meanwhile, high real estate prices and limited supply are boosting demand for multifamily residential real estate, brightening that sector’s medium-term outlook. Slower home construction activity is likely to keep a lid on excess inventory (Display), and support home prices and rental values - even in a more volatile interest-rate environment.

Convergence Underway Across Public, Private Markets

Investors have historically accessed CRE credit exposure through either private lending or public market vehicles such as commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS). Increasingly, private and public channels are converging, creating a more flexible and dynamic investment landscape.

In private markets, investors can originate or provide capital directly to borrowers, often capturing a premium for sourcing, loan structuring and illiquidity. These investments typically offer structural features and attractive income profiles that may reduce mark-to-market volatility.

Public markets can present compelling opportunities during periods of dislocation. As we see it, recent volatility has been driven more by macro uncertainty than by deteriorating fundamentals. That disruption could create attractive entry points for investors.

Single Asset Single Borrower: Best of Bold Worlds?

Positioned between public and private segments is the growing single asset single borrower (SASB) market, where transactions have elements of both public and private investment, including:

  • Access to institutional assets, either individually or through a diversified portfolio
  • Simpler loan structures than the more tailored structures in private markets
  • Liquidity and price transparency associated with public securitized markets

With the potential to combine asset-level underwriting discipline with market-based liquidity, we think SASB deals broaden investors’ toolkits.

Rather than viewing public and private markets as siloes, portfolios can allocate dynamically based on relative value. This may mean emphasizing private lending when spreads are lower and leaning into public markets when volatility creates price dislocations. With SASB structures, meanwhile, investors can access high-quality assets with attractive structures and liquidity.

A Diverse Opportunity Set

Commercial real estate isn’t monolithic, and it’s not defined by the office sector alone. It’s a diverse asset class that spans public and private markets, offering exposure to income-generating physical assets with conservative capital structures that may enhance risk-mitigation potential.

For investors, we think the commercial real estate market adds up to a compelling set of opportunities. Periods of uncertainty can obscure these dynamics. But these are often the moments when relative-value dynamics are most attractive.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla's Terafab Dream
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) had their earnings yesterday, but given the company trades at ~226x NTM EV/EBIT multiple, it is a bit of a waste of time to spend time dissecting their existing business. Ultimately, Tesla has become a moonshot bet on several projects that currently do not contribute materially to their numbers. Full Self Driving, or FSD, Robotaxi, Optimus, and more recently, Terafab are some of their key moonshot projects. It’s not often that I come across companies in public markets which are generously valued purely based on optional value attached to ongoing projects with a high degree of uncertainty attached to it, so it speaks volumes of the level of confidence investors have shown on Elon Musk’s ability to unlock value even from what it may appear to be pretty improbable scenarios.

Even by Tesla’s standards, the Terafab project is perhaps the epitome of Musk’s eternal desire to swing for the fences. So, what exactly is this project about?

Terafab is a planned ~$25 billion semiconductor fab that Elon Musk announced last month as a joint venture between Tesla, SpaceX (SPACE), and xAI (X.AI). The name comes from its headline target: 1 terawatt of annual compute output, which Musk mentioned to be ~50x what all existing global fabs produce today combined. It will target 2nm process technology and an initial capacity of 100,000 wafer starts per month. Almost every aspect of this project screams of a quixotic dream, so I was curious to hear more about Terafab project in yesterday’s call. When asked about Terafab, Elon Musk said the following:

“we’re still working out the details of the Terafab deployment. In the near term, Tesla will be building the research fab on our Giga Texas campus. This is something we expect to be probably a $3 billion-ish initiative and capable of maybe a few thousand wafers per month, but it’s really intended to try out ideas. The research fab, it was in terms of maybe -- we have some ideas for improving the fundamental technology of how chips are made and some new physics we’d like to test out, but we also want to test out the ability to -- to see if something is working in production. So you need kind of like a few thousand wafer starts a month to make sure that a production process is sound.

And then SpaceX is going to take care of like the initial phase of the scaled up Terafab. And that’s what we figured out thus far. I think this will be unique in the world, or at least I’m not aware of any a place where you have the lithography mask creation, and then logic, memory and packaging in under one roof in one building. That’s about the fastest I could possibly imagine doing [ recourse ] of research and development and being able to try out some pretty radical ideas, some of which have -- it’s kind of long-shot stuff, but if some of these long shots pan out, it would be radical improvements in the way [indiscernible] work.”

Indeed, that does sound like a pretty long shot! A conventional leading-edge fab actually handles only wafer fabrication and ASML (ASML) provides the lithography tools. Design happens at fabless customers, memory is made at separate fabs (Micron (MU), Samsung (SSNLF), SK hynix), and advanced packaging and test are typically done at OSAT facilities or dedicated packaging sites. Terafab's claim is that design, lithography, fabrication, memory production, advanced packaging, and testing all happen at a single site. My guess is that anyone other than Elon Musk would not dare propose something like this. But once you've figured out how to catch a returning rocket booster with a pair of mechanical arms, the word "impossible" probably stops meaning what it means to the rest of us. If anything, such consensus of incredulity may have only propelled him to give this a shot. We don’t need to believe this project has any credible path when Musk himself acknowledges the long-shot nature of the bet, but admittedly I’m glad that he’s giving this a go without accepting the “fate” that the semi value chain has reached almost the end state.

To be clear, Musk isn’t doing this because he’s annoyed by the ever-burgeoning profit pool of the players in the semi value chain, but because he thinks the compute need is going to be so great that it would be pretty much impossible to meet demand with the existing ecosystem. From the call yesterday:

Terafab is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers. It's just literally, we don't see a path to having enough efficient quantity of AI chips down the road. As we scale production to high levels, just the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory, it's just doesn't -- we just anticipate hitting a wall if we don't make chips ourselves.

As a reminder, TSMC (TSM) was also asked about Tesla’s Terafab project during their earnings call. They didn’t sound concerned. From the call (emphasis mine):

“…both Intel (INTC) and Tesla, they are TSMC’s customers. But again, they are our competitors, and we view Intel as our formidable competitor and do not underestimate them. But having said that, there are no shortcuts. The fundamental rules of the foundry game never change. They need the technology leadership, manufacturing excellence and customer trust, and most of all, the service, which has been mentioned by Jensen; thank you for his wording.

Again, let me say that it takes 2 to 3 years to build a new fab, no shortcuts. And it takes another 1 to 2 years to ramp it up. Again, that’s the fundamental of foundry industry. And whether we try to win them back, actually, they are still our customer. And we are very confident in our technology position, and we work very hard to capture every piece of business possible.

I will continue to listen to Tesla’s earnings calls to stay updated on their moonshot projects, especially Terafab.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QuantumScape: The Battery Bet Is Risky, But The Upside Just Got Bigger
data.description:
Investment Thesis

QuantumScape Corporation (QS) is pursuing the holy grail of battery technology—a solid-state battery chemistry that promises to revolutionize how the world stores electricity. They're not there yet. However, as highlighted in the previous article, the progress is real, measurable, and validated by heavyweights such as Volkswagen (VWAGY), Murata (MRAAY), and other industry insiders who have committed capital to QS's efforts. The support QS has garnered signals that they might be sitting on something valuable, warranting investors' attention or even a speculative position for those who can afford to take the risk.

Strategically, QS has been flexible. The company changed its business model by adopting a more CapEx-light strategy, licensing its intellectual property and sharing its know-how rather than establishing commercial-scale manufacturing operations itself. This shift is understandable. Manufacturing yields are the graveyard of battery startups, because many concepts that work in the labs don't work in a commercial manufacturing setting. Moreover, by shifting to an IP-licensing model, QS shifts the burden of capacity preparation to its customers. Many large-scale customers have historically demanded extraordinarily large capacities, including redundant facilities, to secure their supply chains, without committing to purchases. This is particularly frustrating for smaller market entrants in the auto industry, where sales leads are long and resources are limited.

Finally, going forward, QS's narrative could be shaped by opportunities beyond the automotive sector, including stationary power storage, light electric vehicles (e-motorcycles, etc.), and drones, where high-density batteries have an edge.

My view is still the same as six months ago: QS is a well-capitalized battery technology company with a significant upside potential in an expanding market.

Market Position - The Bigger Picture

From a market position perspective, QS's strategic and operational shifts aren't exactly trailblazing but rather a confirmation of existing trends I've highlighted in prior articles. For example, the shift to a capital-light business model mimics Solid Power (SLDP), another battery tech company pursuing SSB. From a wider electronics sector point of view, other aspiring auto tier-1 suppliers are shifting away from the auto industry. Prolonged certification, testing, and expensive paths to adoption make the auto industry a challenging nut to crack, and an increasing number of aspiring tier 1 suppliers are targeting the low-hanging fruit in other industries. This includes technologies seeking to introduce new LiDARs and other sensor products.

Automotive remains an important market for QS. Last year, the company's CEO joined representatives from Volkswagen and Ducati (part of Volkswagen) to present the V21L electric motorcycle, the first real-life demonstration of QS's batteries. This year, QS and Ducati will collect real-world data to test the batteries' performance in real-world settings.

However, one can't help but notice that the first application wasn't for EVs, but instead, one e-motorcycle model. This is a signal that automakers are being very careful and taking their time in testing and adopting the new batteries. Naturally, e-motorcycles require fewer battery pouches and cells compared to EVs. Also, the e-motorcycle market, particularly for high-performance e-motorcycles, is small and loss-making. Harley-Davidson spun off its e-motorcycle unit, called LiveWire (LVWR), into a separate, publicly traded company, which has since expanded into "kids" sports via its new e-bike line in an attempt to boost sales. This is not to discourage investors but to build more realistic expectations and to draw an accurate picture of where the company is on its path to commercialization.

Drones and Emerging Applications

In the latest shareholder letter, QS's CEO stated that the company is actively pursuing new markets for its battery technology, emphasizing the safety and energy density of its pouches and cells—features that are particularly important for certain applications such as drones and energy storage.

These emerging sectors provide new target markets for QS. The pace of the AI infrastructure buildout exceeds what utility companies can power, due to the time required to build power transmission lines and supply constraints on utility-grade natural gas turbines. Hyperscalers have implemented creative solutions to the power supply problem, and one of the beneficiaries has been lithium batteries.

The drone market is another attractive opportunity. The increasing use of drones in wars further enhances demand, particularly for high-energy-density batteries. The market is highly competitive, and some battery tech companies have been structurally built around the drone market, including Amprius (AMPX).

Balance Sheet

QS remains in the pre-revenue phase, but it has enough cash to carry it through multiple years of revenue-less development phase. The company ended Q1'26 with ~900 million in cash and equivalents. Debt is negligible, with interest expense standing at less than a million dollars in the quarter. About 31% of the ~$100 million net loss is non-cash SBC expense. Overall, cash burn stood at roughly $70 million during the quarter (including $10 million on CapEx, a rate that fell below depreciation expense of $14 million—a manifestation of QS's shift to a capital-light business model).

The Eagle Manufacturing Line, designed to showcase QS's manufacturing capabilities, is now complete, contributing to a YoY decline in CapEx.

Valuation

QS remains a speculative bet that it will someday carve out a meaningful niche in the lithium battery market. Its partnership with the second-largest car manufacturer, Volkswagen, is key to its valuation hypothesis. Progress has been slow. Hopefully, the company could find less red tape in other industries it is now pursuing, including stationary storage and drones.

The good thing is that its certification work with automotive companies could be used in its pursuit of other applications. The market is huge, worth nearly ~$70 billion as of December 2025, and expected to grow to $300 billion according to the latest estimates by Grand View Research. This is a huge TAM for a company with a $4.5 billion market cap. For example, a modest 5% market share would translate to $3.5 and $15 billion in sales for QS, based on market estimates for 2025 and 2030, respectively.

Final Thoughts

QS remains a speculative play in the battery technology market. The news that the company is exploring new markets is positive for its share price. The prolonged automotive certification process has frustrated many investors. I believe there might be commercial factors that are delaying adoption, including existing infrastructure and battery programs that Volkswagen isn't yet ready to replace. It is likely that investors might have to wait longer. The key concern is the dynamic nature of the market, characterized by rapid technological changes. The tide could turn against the company before it reaches the commercial stage.

The solid balance sheet plays in QS's favor, giving it the ability to fund multiple years of pre-revenue operations. As of December 2025, the company held about $900 million in cash, weighed against roughly $280 million in annual cash burn (a run rate calculated based on Q4'25 cash burn). Investors should expect volatility. The thesis hasn't changed since I last penned my first piece covering QS about six months ago. What changed is that the ticker is down by about 50%. If anything, this strengthens the thesis for QuantumScape Corporation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: The AI-Driven Structural Revaluation Unlocks Further Upside
data.description:
Intro

I’d assume most readers would agree that it’s hard to find something genuinely original when writing about Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). It's not just about being original, but offering value to both the writer and the readers, as nearly every angle seems to have been thoroughly discussed.

I think there is little value in repeating yet again some well-worn topic or rewriting recent earnings reports—that has been done fairly well already, and readers can choose from dozens of such analyses.

I still found that while there has been some discussion — though not too much yet — more broadly about how to value memory makers going forward, that might be the angle worth exploring for me with Micron this time. I also agree that recent earnings and short‑term estimates could be a bit less relevant now than the longer‑term themes, since we already know that the next year or two will be somewhat distorted by supply constraints.

I think the CEO’s response at the latest earnings call to the analyst’s question about ongoing supply constraints offers valuable insight into the matter:

What we have said is in the last earnings call that some of our key customers are -- we are able to fulfill only 50% to two-thirds of their demand in the medium term. And yes, that still remains the case.

And the topic was discussed further, providing more color on how long the constrained situation could last:

We continue to expect supply-demand conditions for both DRAM and NAND to remain tight beyond calendar 2026.

Is the Valuation Narrative for Memory Makers, Often Viewed as Commodity-like Cyclical Businesses, Shifting?

Up until recently, the memory segment has been widely covered and, one could argue, even viewed as a fairly boring, commodity‑like cyclical business.

When the economy is booming, people buy more devices — gadgets, phones, laptops — and those devices need memory, allowing producers to sell at reasonable prices and solid margins because demand is strong. During upcycles, memory makers invest heavily in boosting capacity, only to see some of it underutilized when the market shifts into a downcycle. To keep fabs running, producers cut prices, margins collapse, and losses follow. In my view, that broad pattern describes what Micron’s business looked like until quite recently.

Well‑known global audit powerhouse PricewaterhouseCoopers has described the situation in their latest research - Semiconductor and beyond; Global semiconductor industry outlook 2026 as follows:

The memory market is famous for boom-and-bust “super-cycles.” A wave typically starts when a new platform—smartphones, cloud servers, AI accelerators—soaks up capacity, sending prices and margins soaring. Usually, suppliers over-build, inventories swell and a down-cycle follows.

For me, the next question is whether the traditional narrative of endlessly repeating cycles is beginning to change, and if the market has already started to factor in this shift. Micron emphasizes that it sees no signs of demand slowing, and that the rapid development of AI is actually driving it higher. The company argues it needs to invest more, and as quickly as possible, just to keep up with accelerating demand. The next couple of years aren’t even up for debate at this point — they’re essentially sold out.

During the latest earnings call, Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra highlighted the crucial role of memory in the AI era, describing it as a key defining asset of AI’s evolution:

Our memory and storage solutions are at the heart of this AI revolution. Memory makes AI smarter and more capable, enabling longer context windows, deeper reasoning chains and multi-agent orchestration.

As AI evolves, we expect compute architectures to become more memory intensive. This is why we strongly believe that Micron is one of the biggest beneficiaries and enablers of AI. AI hasn't just increased demand for memory, it has fundamentally recast memory as a defining strategic asset in the AI era.

It seems to me that a strategic asset for AI, especially in the early stages of true growth, can’t be something that cycles like a commodity.

PwC offers its perspective on how future cycles may unfold:

Whether future cycles will moderate or not is up for debate. One camp believes tighter supply chain analytics, shorter equipment lead-times and HBM-style node diversification could smooth price swings. The other points to explosive AI workloads, autonomous vehicle data capture and edge computing growth as fresh demand shocks that may spark the next upturn. Emerging use cases such as mixed-reality headsets and smart industry sensors also underline memory’s central role.

Having recently analyzed Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest GTC 2026 event—where the emphasis was on the interface and the next wave of real-world use cases—I align with the latter view: a renewed demand shock seems a quite realistic scenario. I believe that over the next five years, we are unlikely to see any issues on the demand side, barring a major macroeconomic slowdown. This does not imply I expect demand to weaken after that timeframe; rather, I think real-world developments change in such a way that predicting too far ahead becomes unreliable.

PwC presents its perspective on the evolution of cycles:

In practice, the depth of each cycle may hinge on three variables: the speed at which breakthrough applications ramp, the scale of manufacturers’ capacity bets and the pace of process migrations that boost bits per wafer. Tracking those indicators—and adjusting investment accordingly— will likely be critical to navigating whatever wave comes next.

…and their research offers an HBM market size forecast that predicts consistent growth over the next five years, without any anticipated setbacks:

They also share they perspective on how AI is driving increased demand for HBM:

The surge of generative-AI training and inference has turned high-bandwidth memory (HBM) into a critical component of modern data center servers. CPU, GPU and accelerator performance keeps climbing, but if the attached memory cannot supply data fast enough the entire workload can experience bottleneck.

HBM tackles that bottleneck by stacking multiple DRAM dies on a tiny base-logic die, connecting them with through-silicon vias (TSVs) and mounting the stack on a silicon interposer right next to the compute chip. This 3D integration delivers terabytes-per-second bandwidth in a very small footprint, making HBM crucial for power-hungry AI and HPC systems.

In my view, this further reinforces the idea that the market is undergoing a structural change in how it values Micron and its peers moving forward.

I believe the following chart provides further support for the shifting thesis. It shows that until late 2023, the cyclical waves were fairly well-defined and similar at their core: during an upcycle, Micron’s gross profit rose alongside revenue, the fundamental valuation multiple (EV/EBITDA) moved lower, and the stock price remained relatively stable without major spikes. The market was essentially pricing in the next downcycle.

The chart also shows that things changed in late 2023 and early 2024, when the stock price began to trend modestly higher — and pushed the EV/EBITDA multiple sharply upward — even though memory demand was weak, the downcycle was fully in place, and the company’s financial results well reflected that. As we remember, early 2024 marked the real beginning of the so‑called AI revolution. I think it clearly illustrates that the market anticipated the coming ‘perfect demand storm’ driven by AI.

Furthermore, the chart now shows that the previous patterns have broken: the stock price is rising in parallel with the jump in gross margin and revenue, suggesting that the market does not expect a traditional downcycle and stock decline this time. While the EV/EBITDA multiple has come down from the initial spike, as of now, it has remained relatively elevated — near its historical peak of around 10.

And although it doesn’t show up in the chart yet, if we take the next quarter’s outlook into account, all trend lines continue moving higher together, while EV/EBITDA — based on my calculations, given the forward EBITDA trends toward $100 billion — would move well below the 10× level.

I think the main conclusions from this chart are the following: first, the market initially predicted the upcoming AI-driven cycle remarkably well; and second, the old patterns that worked fairly reliably over the past decade have now been disrupted. Whether this means that the valuation assumptions shaped by ever‑repeating cycles are now in the rearview mirror, I don’t know — but I would assume that, from the market’s perspective, something has clearly changed.

What the Shift Means for Micron’s Valuation

As promised, I’ll skip over the latest earnings and just give a quick reminder that it was beat and rise, something that has become quite common lately.

…and the outlook was as presented:

The following points add essential context to the outlook:

I believe the PwC research could be cited once more, as they also provided their estimate regarding the current industrywide supply constraints:

Many suppliers are rushing to add capacity, yet shortages can still appear. First, demand may outrun even the most optimistic forecasts, and the 18-to-24-month lead time for new TSV lines leaves little room for quick fixes. Second, the supply chain is only as strong as its weakest link: limited availability of silicon interposers, advanced bumping capacity, or the specialized base-logic die can all throttle overall HBM output. Until packaging infrastructure scales in parallel with DRAM wafer starts, tight HBM supply—and premium pricing—are likely to persist

I think that if we believe the era of memory‑industry super‑cycles is over — or at least no longer treated as the essential baseline — the valuation assessment becomes quite straightforward. Given that MU currently has zero net debt and, in fact, an excess and growing cash pile, the forward P/E becomes the most relevant multiple for evaluating the valuation perspective. This is even more true considering that the next two years are widely viewed as relatively low‑risk, supply‑driven years with a high degree of forecast visibility.

Cash and debt dynamics:

From my perspective, a forward P/E ratio of 5–8 for a top‑tier AI‑exposed tech company is clearly far too low. Even if we assume that memory sector companies will still be valued to some degree as cyclical businesses, a conservative forward-looking P/E ratio of around 10 would still be more appropriate in this scenario.

Compared to peers like Seagate (STX), Western Digital (WDC), Sandisk (SNDK), and Marvell (MRVL), all MU multiples are lower than the group as well:

I rate Micron as a Buy at these levels.

Risks

Nearly a month ago, Micron sold off alongside its industry peers after news broke that Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) had developed new algorithms ("TurboQuant") designed to reduce the amount of memory required to run LLM models. That’s certainly one of the important risks: the amount of memory needed is high and rising, it is priced high, and AI hyperscalers are actively looking for ways to ease that burden.

Another major risk is the old‑fashioned one: overinvestment, which has historically been a recurring issue in the industry. All major players are currently investing heavily, and it remains understandably difficult to predict at the exact level how technologies and demand will evolve over the long run. In such an environment, it is easy to slip into an overcapacity situation.

Given the geopolitical uncertainties, the risk of recession stays relatively elevated. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, which implies heightened inflationary pressure — and, if prolonged, could evolve into a stagflationary environment.

Takeaway

It seems that Micron — and the broader memory industry — are in the midst of a fundamental shift in how they are being valued going forward. Growing evidence suggests that the era of so‑called supercycles may be giving way to a more stable business model, bringing the sector into closer alignment with the broader semiconductor industry. This transition is being driven by rising structural demand, making the industry less dependent on purely cyclical dynamics.

Such industry‑wide transitional repricing periods often create rare opportunities for outsized returns. While it is understandable that some investors hesitate to buy into a stock that has already risen more than six‑fold in a single year, the rapid pace of the AI evolution is exceptional as well. In my view, the narrative still supports a strong case that Micron’s stock has substantial room to grow.

data.title: Capital Southwest: A 10% Dividend Yield, Plus Stock Price Appreciation Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While as an investor I generally pursue more GARP opportunities, and as an analyst I haven’t covered the financials much, Capital Southwest (CSWC) really stood out on multiple fronts. Capital Southwest is a company with healthy financials, growing relatively fast, trading at a discount, and paying a dividend yield over 10%. This should be enough to catch any investor’s attention, but the inherent growth and business capabilities of the company could further enhance returns beyond the annual 10% yield.

The continuation of monetary de-tightening in the US for this year and next year, after the potential confirmation of a new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, could provide a boost to private equity activity, particularly the lower middle market, which is the most affected by tight financing conditions. But the loosening of credit conditions is in doubt, primarily because of the uncertainty related to the Strait of Hormuz and consequently the oil price. If the high oil prices last for longer, a delayed monetary easing could also delay the expected boost in private equity transactions and, hence, in growth opportunities for Capital Southwest. Due to this macro uncertainty, I have to refrain from rating this stock a Strong Buy and will, for my initial coverage, go with a Buy rating.

Business Overview And Hard Figures

A short overview of the company: Capital Southwest has actually been regulated as a BDC since 1988. A BDC is a type of investment vehicle that invests in private companies, specifically in SMEs, and usually pays a high dividend yield (it needs to pay over 90% of the taxable income to avoid taxation). Historically, the idea was to boost the middle market, but it has since turned into probably one of the best ways for retail investors to gain access to the private equity segment.

Perhaps the most attractive part of the business model of Capital Southwest is its extremely low fixed cost base. The whole business with its $2.1 billion balance sheet (as of 31 December 2025, according to the February 2026 investor presentation) is run with only 36 employees from an office in Dallas, TX. The company boasts an impressive $3.44 million of net income generated per employee and a net income margin of over 45%. The senior management also shows high longevity, ensuring continuation of the business model, with four of the five senior managers in office for at least 10 years, including the CEO, Michael Sarner, who's been in place since 2015. As an investor, especially if I am looking for an income opportunity, the stable setting sounds reassuring to me.

Likewise, the investment strategy has remained relatively steady: Capital Southwest's primary goal is to provide flexible financing solutions from $5 to $50 million each, targeting LTV of 25% to 50% to companies with EBITDA between $3 and $25 million. With a weighted average IRR of 13% on its 189 mid-market companies, the strategy of providing loans to low-leverage, profitable mid-market companies has been, in my opinion, very successful.

Hard Figures

The business efficiency combined with a solid strategy has translated, in my view, to attractive hard figures that make this company potentially undervalued. Indeed, in the earnings report relative to calendar Q4 2025, ending December 31, 2025 (or fiscal Q3 2026), the quarterly pre-tax income per share amounted to $0.60 (in line with expectations). The company announced a dividend of $0.58 per share per quarter, paid monthly ($0.1934 per month), giving the stock a forward dividend yield of over 10% annually based on a $23.5 per share price. In addition to the regular dividend payments, the company also pays from time to time a supplemental dividend, with the last example in calendar Q1 2026 with a $0.06 supplement per share.

The table above is a great visual to understand the consistency but also the growth of the dividend payments since Covid. The company has been paying $0.58 in regular dividends for seven quarters in a row, the longest streak without a hike. I expect, therefore, a hike very soon. But still, assuming no hike and a $0.06 supplemental dividend per share, the dividend yield for the stock based on the closing price of $23.6 lies around 10.8%, which is an attractive, low-risk return for any investor, in my view.

With a cash cushion of $42.6 million (far surpassing current liabilities of $2.3 million) as of December 31, 2025, and high profitability metrics discussed earlier, the stock is, in my view, a safe and consistent dividend payer and even a grower over the years. There are many alternatives, so I will compare only with the main BDC peers that have higher dividend yields than our Capital Southwest: Crescent Capital BDC Inc. (CCAP), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), and Fidus Investment (FDUS).

While all these BDCs have higher dividend yields, Capital Southwest has in total brought more returns in the last three years thanks to share price appreciation relative to peers. The reason is pretty simple, actually: while all these stocks, including Capital Southwest, trade within a narrow PE range of 8-10x (both trailing and forward), not a single one of them has double-digit growth rates. In fact, they range from -15.2% for Crescent Capital BDC to +7.9% for Hercules Capital. Capital Southwest's year-over-year revenue growth stands at +15.61% as of the March 2026 estimate and is expected to grow another +10.87% by March 2027. Therefore, I believe that Capital Southwest is well positioned to continue outperforming, and on top of the 10.8% dividend yield, it is reasonable to expect share price growth of at least 10% following revenue growth of at least 10%, maintaining its 5-year average P/S of 5.5x to 6x, and thus potentially leading to a total return of 20%+ within the next twelve months.

Risk Factor To My Case

The key risk factor that I mentioned in my investment thesis, and which causes me to pick a Buy rating over a Strong Buy, despite an attractive risk-reward ratio, is of a macro nature. With the events currently happening in Iran severely disrupting supply chains once again, it is at this stage still unclear if the Fed will pause, tighten, or ease in the near future. The change of Fed Chair has added to uncertainty, with some mixed signals given. Kevin Warsh has historically spoken against easing but has been picked by President Trump, who has been pressuring for rate cuts. The private equity sector has proven to be dynamic, with continued opportunities for Capital Southwest to underwrite loans to mid-market companies. However, the lack of visibility for an extended period of time could slow private equity activity and Southwest Capital’s double-digit growth rates. I may consider upgrading to a Strong Buy once the Fed Chair has been confirmed and the oil supply chains in the Middle East have been reestablished.

Bottom Line

Capital Southwest offers attractive, consistent dividend payments with a dividend yield of 10.8%, including the expected supplemental dividends. The top-line growth rate is a key enhancer of outperformance relative to peers in terms of total returns, and I believe investors could expect potential total annual returns in 2026 north of 20%. The downside risk is very limited with an efficient, highly profitable business model led by a senior management team that has, for the most part, been in a senior role for over a decade. For all these reasons, I am rating the stock a Buy, just shy of a Strong Buy due to monetary policy uncertainty and hence current lack of visibility within the private equity sector.

data.title: Progress Against Pancreatic Cancer - Part 1
data.description:
We have some really interesting progress in pancreatic cancer to talk about, both on the small-molecule and the mRNA vaccine fronts. Let’s do the small-molecule ones first, because those were honestly more unexpected.

So to lead off, Revolution Medicines (RVMD) announced at the AACR meeting in San Diego that their drug daraxonrasib showed strong efficacy in patients with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). That is a very, very difficult to treat population - these people typically have only a few months to live. But the drug seems to have doubled their expected lifespan (13.2 months overall survival versus 6.7 months for chemotherapy standard of care). That is an extraordinary improvement, and I don’t believe that the field has ever seen anything like it in such patients with such advanced disease.

The first question is, what’s this molecule? Daraxonrasib is shown below, and you would be right to assume that its mechanism of action is not simple. It targets the RAS protein, which has been a white whale of oncology for decades. It was long considered undruggable, but there are now approved drugs that target a rare mutant form of it (G12C) that provides a cysteine handle that can be covalently modified. The problem with those is that: (1) it’s not a common mutation, and (2) it appears relatively easy for tumors to mutate out of this approach.

But daraxonrasib’s mechanism is to stabilize a complex between all sorts of RAS isoforms and the widely expressed chaperone protein cyclophilin A. It was designed in a project starting from a natural product (Sanglifehrin A) that binds to CycA, cutting that down to a minimal binding region (that hydrazide part) and then building back with a covalent warhead to target that G12C residue in the cyclophilin-RAS complex. Some of this work goes back to the Warp Drive Bio days, actually. Further work led to RMC-7977 which accomplishes this feat on a much wider variety of RAS proteins, and then to daraxonrasib itself (RMC-6236).

You’d have to classify this as a molecular glue, I’d say - cyclophilin A doesn’t really have any affinity for RAS under normal conditions, and neither does the drug. But the induced complex has a binding pocket made from surfaces of both proteins that binds the ligand, and this new complex interferes greatly with RAS’s downstream activities. As you’ll see from that last link, optimizing this structure for that binding mode while giving it good enough pharmacokinetic properties to make it a drug was also not trivial (!)

PDAC is known to be one of the most “RAS-addicted” tumor types, so it’s a natural place to try this compound out. That survival benefit is impossible to argue with, but going after RAS does not come without penalties. The company says that the drug was generally well-tolerated, but that’s on the oncology scale. Former senator Ben Sasse says that he had been noticing increasing back and abdominal pain, and unfortunately found that that was due to inoperable Stage 4 pancreatic cancer which had already metastatized to liver, lung, and other tissues. If you’re up to the point of overt physical symptoms like that with PDAC, it’s almost certainly bad news; this is one of the cancer types that famously sneaks up on people and is notoriously difficult to catch early.

He’s been on daraxonrasib since early this year, and describes it this way: “... it’s a nasty drug. It causes crazy stuff like my body can’t grow skin and so I bleed all out of a whole bunch of parts of me that shouldn’t be bleeding.” If you go to that link above, be prepared, because he also looks like he’s had aqua regia thrown all over him (and apparently feels a bit like that, too). But his tumor volume has gone down by about 75%, and there’s a very strong chance that he wouldn’t still be alive at all without having gone on the drug. He seems to feel that it’s a worthwhile tradeoff, and I suspect that there will be many others in his situation who agree.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: It Only Needs To Get Less Bad
data.description:
The Pittsburgh-based company The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC), after recently pausing the split of its business between Kraft and Heinz, is an investment thesis much less about growth and much more about stabilization. The market continues to price in ongoing deterioration of the business, especially in the U.S. (the main market), reflected in very depressed multiples and negative revisions in both the top and bottom line.

But today, my bullish view of Kraft Heinz (KHC) goes against the muted consensus on the stock.

With the focus of the new management team on execution, proposing a new turnaround (another one), but this time potentially more aggressive, this pivot does not need to work fully. At current levels, any sign that the business is deteriorating at a slower pace could already be enough to surprise excessively pessimistic estimates and unlock some re-rating. And Q1 results to be released in early May should be the first test of this.

A Decade of Losing Relevance

Kraft-Heinz has been a chronically disappointing stock from virtually every angle over the past decade, at least. And I would say that the classic problem that plagues the company is not entirely the rise of GLP-1 drugs; it is not the macro issue in isolation, it is not the consumer eating less processed foods, but something much simpler (and more serious): execution and relevance.

KHC has failed to do what it needs to do most, which is to gain shelf space for consumers, especially in the U.S. This is because of a confluence of errors, which I would list as (1) lack of relevant innovation; (2) reliance on "nostalgic" brands; (3) weak in-store execution; (4) misaligned pricing (expensive vs. perceived value); and (5) years of underinvestment.

The result was 10 years of losing market share in the U.S., virtually zero organic growth, and a portfolio of brands that are strong in name only, but are little desired.

Turning Around… Without Turning

After a decade of "donating" share to the competition, it is clearly not the first time that the Kraft-Heinz management team has come up with a turnaround plan. So it is reasonable that the market is quite skeptical of the talk of pivoting the business to stop the loss of share, especially in the U.S.

But first, I think it's interesting to go through a timeline of recent "turnaround ideas" to try to find answers to whether it is plausible to believe that the 2026 turnaround will be successful.

Starting with the "reinvest to grow" phase between 2019 and 2021. This phase came after several years of aggressive cost cuts under the 3G model, at a time when brands were already weakened and a gigantic impairment of ~$15 billion. The plan was along the "generic" lines of increasing investment in marketing, reinvesting in brands, improving product quality and so forth. It turned out that the execution proved to be inconsistent, and the innovation was weak and was very incremental. It was still clear that the "cost-cutting" culture was still rooted in Kraft Heinz. Growth in this period was meager, and the share continued to fall.

Then came 2022, with the post-pandemic environment, where the new turnaround plan came along the lines of "digital push". The result was a lot of framework, little delivery, and still high operational complexity.

So in 2023, Kraft Heinz's management team pushed for a pricing-led strategy, where rising inflation led the company to aggressively raise product prices. This proved to be great in the short term, as there was an immediate gain in margins and revenues. But of course, it was short-lived. The caveat was that structurally volumes fell and consumers migrated en masse to private label.

Lastly, between 2023 and 2025, there was no major explicit turnaround plan, but rather something quieter with a focus on growth via pricing — price increases to pass on inflation and more efficient promotions. Pretty much reflected the same DNA from the 3G Capital time, with a focus again on cutting costs and optimizing the supply chain.

Innovation remained largely incremental with line extensions, small improvements, and some occasional launches. In a way, it worked well in terms of margin expansion and solid cash generation. But it didn't solve the problem of perceived brand value. And Kraft Heinz continued to lose share.

Why This Time Might Be Different

For 2026, now the management team under new CEO Steve Cahillane is trying to sell a more "aggressive" version of the turnaround attempts in the past. Investing more, revitalizing brands, and improving execution continue to be the pillars. The CEO himself was direct in saying that "We can't rely on old ads and nostalgia anymore".

But what seems different now is that management itself has seemed to recognize that there was a problem of underinvestment in the past. And with that, it's guiding ~$600 million of incremental investment this time. The idea is to increase R&D by 20%, and marketing spend rising to ~5.5% of sales versus 4.9% in 2025. In addition, there will be an increase in the sales team to close this gap with peers.

The idea is to replicate in the U.S. some of Kraft Heinz's few specific recent success stories. In Canada, for example, the company removed operational complexity by focusing on a few "big bets" and saw ~4% revenue CAGR in the last three years and market share gain. In the UK, Heinz Beans (reformulated) recovered share after ten years of losses, preserving margins, with a new pricing/pack architecture, and in emerging markets, Kraft Heinz grows at 13% YoY organically, with distribution also expanding 13% YoY.

Perhaps more importantly, it seems to me that the new management team seems less obsessed with portfolio engineering (splitting the business) as Mr. Cahillane has already arrived, pausing the split that was well underway.

“The work to separate the business is so enormous… and the work to turn around a business that is declining is also an enormous task. Doing both of those things at the same time is very difficult, if not impossible, to do the way that you want to do them.”

The Bar Is Exceptionally Low

Truth be told, the market remains far from buying the growth turnaround for KHC, even though it may be more aggressive than the previous ones.

Based on FY26 guidance, KHC still expects the year to bring negative organic sales growth between -3.5% to -1.5% (vs. a negative 3.4% organic net sales in FY25), adjusted EPS between $1.89 to $2.10 vs. $2.60 in FY25.

Looking beyond FY26, where in theory it should be the most capital-intensive year of the turnaround plan, revenue estimates are practically flat from ~$24.4 billion to ~$25.4 billion by 2030, implying a paltry growth of 0% to 1% per year. Revisions to these numbers have also been consistently negative over the past three to six months, clearly pricing in stagnation.

Even worse, when we look at the EPS consensus, the outlook is for a 21% YoY decline, with revisions of 18% to 19% in the last three and six months, respectively. The interpretation of this is that the ~$600 million to be poured into investments should not only add practically zero to the top line but will also pressure margins in the short and medium term.

Against what could already be considered a conservative consensus, especially for revenues, for the last three consecutive years, Kraft Heinz has failed to beat market estimates.

Turning to dividends, KHC's thesis, however, draws attention with a dividend yield today of ~7.3%, and with estimates pointing to maintaining these levels for the next three years — implying a stable payout. In my view, the market not appreciating this robust level of shareholder returns goes directly along the line of lack of growth and risk.

It is also noteworthy that Kraft Heinz trades at a discount to its major peers through the EV/EBIT lens, trading at a mere ~9.4x, the lowest levels in the last five years. That being said, I don't believe that based on all the disasters in expectations and the execution of the KHC thesis over the last several years, it is possible to nail down that this is finally a deep value territory for this stock.

Therefore, I believe that a consistent multiple re-rating for Kraft Heinz stock will only come if the market sees some evidence that a slowdown in market share loss is happening.

Final Remarks

Nearly everything conspires against Kraft Heinz's bullish thesis in the present and eventually future, from what the consensus is expecting. I am pleased, however, that the 2026 turnaround plan seems to be more aggressive than the previous ones, although it is still very slow in tangible progress. I believe that this is the only chance that exists today for a more bullish prognosis in the company. I don't think recessions or valuations in technical deep value territories can make any difference to stock sentiment if no progress is seen towards normalizing market share loss.

As KHC is about to report its earnings on May 6, in addition to posting slightly better numbers than the -19% YoY decline forecast in EPS and the -2% YoY revenue, I would celebrate here any news like "North America organic net sales declined ~6% YoY vs. ~6.5% last year".

At current multiples, small signs of improvement like this could be enough for a short-term rebound, in my view. As the main risks in the short term, I would place the conflict in the Middle East, which has exerted a clear pressure on KHC stock price, fuel price hikes increase pressure on consumers and thus increase price sensitivity—which in theory tends to accelerate the migration to private label and put even more pressure on volumes in categories where Kraft Heinz has already been losing relevance. In the long run, the stagnation of the turnaround plan materializing as a base case scenario could make the ~9.4x EBIT multiple still demanding.

The bottom line is that I am adopting a position contrary to what the markets have been pricing, anticipating that any small advances in share normalization may surprise, albeit modestly, the uber bearish estimates present in the spreadsheets of sell-side analysts.

data.title: Heineken: Volume Inflection And Capital Returns Reinforce The Bull Case
data.description:
In our last update, we were not lucky with the timing, given the escalation in the Middle East.

That said, we report positively on Heineken N.V. (HEINY)'s 2025 performance. In our view, Heineken’s 2026 outlook appeared appropriately conservative, and with the EverGreen 2030 strategy, we remained supportive of the equity story. Following a 12.3% decline since mid-February (Fig. 1), we see upside potential.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

The company reported volume growth of plus +2.8% (Fig. 2), ahead of Wall Street's expectation of +2.3%. Resilient volumes and a favorable price MIX mainly supported this. In numbers, price MIX contributed +3%, backed by the Americas and APAC. From a GEO perspective (Fig. 3), APAC stood out for volume growth (+3.7%), driven by strong momentum in Vietnam and continued market share gains. India also sees favorable comps due to better weather. Africa and the Middle East also delivered growth, while the Americas underperformed, with volumes down 2.4%. This was due to softer trends in Mexico and Brazil, where pricing and a shorter Carnival season weighed on performance.

Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

We reviewed Heineken’s latest annual report and found that exposure in the Middle East is very limited and not a core driver of the investment case. The “Middle East” component in reporting is bundled with key markets such as Nigeria, South Africa, and Ethiopia. On an indirect basis, developments in the Middle East can still affect the company through higher energy costs (for glass, logistics, and brewing inputs) and subdued global consumer sentiment. We saw little mention of the ongoing escalation, and with the guidance confirmation, we are confident.

Going back to the business, Heineken's trading update provides a powerful confirmation that its "EverGreen 2030" strategy is delivering tangible results. This is the first quarter with a critical inflection point - a return to positive organic volume growth. This was driven by exceptional strength in the premium portfolio. In numbers, the portfolio grew by 5.8%, demonstrating consumer "trade up," which is far more profitable and sustainable than simply raising prices on mainstream products.

Here at the Lab, we believe Wall Street is pessimistic about the company's volume growth. This is demonstrated by the company's beat in the latest trading update. Management’s decision to maintain its outlook should be viewed as a position of strength. There are external risks, yet they simultaneously signal that their internal strategy is robust enough to navigate them.

We also report the positive FIFCO's acquisition in Costa Rica. This is a key market for Heineken, while the company announced the disposal of operations in the Democratic Republic of Congo. This is a clear discipline to exit markets without a clear path to leadership and profitability.

As anticipated in our Fiscal Year 2025 results comment, the company announced a second €750 million buyback tranche. Heineken also increased its dividend payout ratio from 30-40% to 30-50% (based on net profit). In our view, these are positive changes with a clear intention to remunerate shareholders. And, as a result, a reflection within the management team on Heineken's cash flow sustainability.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Given solid Q1 results and better-than-anticipated volume growth, we slightly increase our 2026 sales target from €30.8 billion to €30.9 billion. On a conservative basis, we continue to use core operating profit growth at the company's mid guidance. In numbers, Heineken expects EBIT margin growth of 2%-6%. Therefore, we estimate an EBIT at €4.85 billion, with a core operating profit margin of 15.6%. This is still the Q1 update, and it is a bit early to raise our expectations. Given that we have already implied the second buyback tranche, there is no change in our interest payments or corporate tax. These costs are estimated at 3.5% with a €600 million payment in 2026, and at 27-28% with a €1.3 billion outflow for corporate tax. Given the usual extraordinary items, our 2026 income reached €2.93 billion.

Considering the current shares outstanding and including the additional buyback, our EPS is set at €5.26. With an unchanged P/E of 20x, we increase our valuation to €105.2 per share ($61.5 in ADR). As a reminder, the Heineken ADR price and the Heineken euro-listed share price are linked by a simple conversion. In numbers, 1 ADR, Heineken N.V. (HEINY) is equal to 0.5 ordinary shares listed in Amsterdam (Euronext).

Risks

We have long covered Heineken, so our downside risks include higher energy costs due to the Middle East crisis. This might also provide a consumer slowdown. Here at the Lab, we also see risks arising from changes in consumer preferences. In our last update, we also reported the "Dry January" risks, which is a campaign encouraging people to abstain from alcohol for the 31 days of January to reset their relationship with drinking. In Q1, the company was impacted by €182 million or 2.8% due to the Euro strengthening. The main impacts were related to the Vietnamese Dong, Indian Rupee, and the Ethiopian Birr. Other risks to consider might include limited marketing, stricter regulation of alcoholic products, and higher tax rates.

Conclusion

The company's Q1 results demonstrate solid progress on the EverGreen 2030 strategy and higher shareholder remuneration. Our EPS was slightly better due to a lower share number, but the supportive volume in Q1 makes this company a buy at this price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon Earnings Preview: Q1 2026
data.description:
Amazon Q1 earnings preview: What’s happening to margins?

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues for North America of $102.1 billion expected for Q1 have edged upward since the October release, driven by resilience in Amazon's (AMZN) online retail business. Expectations for both North American and International online retail segments have remained stable since last quarter. Consensus for AWS also has remained around $36.8 billion. The focus will likely be on the Q1 2026 performance and Q2 2026 outlook for the online retail and AWS margins and their impact on EPS growth.

The North America retail operating profit margin has increased significantly from losses a few years ago to an estimated 6.5% for Q1 2025, a 100bps decline since late January. For Q1, the estimated margin expectations have widened substantially to a range of 0.9% to 7.8%, with consensus at 6.5%. The International operating profit margin range is more extreme at 0.2% to 14.7%, with consensus settling at 3.2%. The debate about the impact of Middle East conflict and tariffs is captured in the significant range of margin estimates for these segments.

The AWS margin for Q1 is expected to be 35.7%. It has come down from the 37.7% level expected in October. The range of estimates for the Q1 AWS margin has also widened to 30.9% to 40.0% with consensus at 35.7%, reflecting debate about the continued demand for AI and cloud services.

We are closely watching how the company will guide Q2 2026 and what the company will say about its investments into AI, as Amazon’s FY 2026 CapEx numbers have continued to increase. According to consensus projections, CapEx estimates have expanded almost 4x from $52.7 billion in FY 2023 to currently nearly $200 billion in FY 2026. Given the current backdrop and increasing energy prices, there are questions about whether the Company will maintain its full-year CapEx guidance.

The stock has traded up 4% since last quarter. The consensus P/E for 2027 is 26x and a target price of $285. Could the Q1 release provide a catalyst for the stock?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: 4 Key Themes On Why This Earnings Report Is Critical
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The next earnings report from Alphabet (GOOGL) is due on April 29, 2026, with expectations running high. One of the main questions on investors’ minds is how to assess the cost of maintaining leadership during a period of aggressive growth in the AI industry. With the company’s management planning to spend up to $185 billion on capital expenditures in 2026, a number of investors have doubts about the wisdom of such investments. While I believe the risks of declining business margins are higher than before, these risks are entirely offset by the opportunities emerging around GOOGL. One feature of the company’s current strategy is the development of a new vertically integrated system, including not only an agency cloud but also in-house chip manufacturing.

I will discuss the key questions that the company’s management is likely to address in its upcoming earnings report. Not only will I outline each question, however, but we will also explore the most likely outcomes of what we’ll see in the report. Please forgive the spoiler, yet in my view, it is likely that the market will be in for another "bullish" surprise.

Capital Expenditures vs. Margins

My recent analysis of Microsoft (MSFT) pointed out the main risk facing the software giant, namely the return on investment. The company has no guarantee that the massive capital expenditures allocated to building AI infrastructure will pay off as originally planned. On the other hand, the situation with GOOGL is different, though the risk is equally significant, since it could affect the company’s profitability. When compared to MSFT, for example, Alphabet has an advantage in adapting its business for further improvement through the development of AI capabilities. This is because GOOGL does not require an aggressive transformation of its business model, whereas its computing infrastructure has a higher probability of being 100% loaded with highly efficient products for customers.

This raises an important question: what impact will CapEx of up to $185 billion have on the company’s key profitability metric—EPS? Analysts’ consensus forecast suggests that the next earnings report will reveal a decline in EPS from $2.81 to $2.63. One of the main reasons for this is precisely the increase in capital expenditures. The GOOGL management needs to provide solid evidence that these investments are justified by growing demand, not just a desire to join the AI race. It is interesting to note that all of the last 12 earnings reports have delivered a positive surprise in terms of EPS. That is why I believe that this time, as well, the chances are very high that we will see actual results exceeding analysts’ consensus estimates.

Google Cloud Gains Momentum

The other day, Alphabet announced a $1 billion contract with Merck (MRK) under which the pharmaceutical company will be supplied with Google Cloud services to automate all its business processes. Other reports of new cloud service clients emerged on the same day. Examples include Thinking Machines Labs and consumer goods giant Mars. This type of news comes out regularly, but it's not surprising, because this product segment has the greatest potential for sales growth.

As part of the next earnings report, attention should be paid to the change in the cloud segment’s share of GOOGL’s revenue mix. It is expected that cloud service sales will increase by approximately 50% compared to last year. Another important factor is the volume of outstanding contracts, whose value previously reached a record $240 billion. I believe we will see continued growth in this metric. Comments on sales in the EU could provide additional insight into the cloud segment, since a "sovereign cloud" deal was recently concluded. It is important, partly because of the EU’s recognition that GOOGL’s cloud services are secure for institutional-level clients.

Gemini Monetization and YouTube/Search Sustainability

Alphabet's most promising product is Gemini, whose monthly active user base has reached 750 million. However, there is a question of how this scale can be translated into quality—specifically, an increase in product margins. As investors, it is important for us to receive confirmation that the integration of AI into the search system will not cannibalize advertising revenue. The goal of the company is to improve search effectiveness, reflected in CTR metrics. But, in addition to its own monetization, I'd like to hear comments from GOOGL management on how they assess competition from Meta (META).

Forecasts by eMarketer highlight the potential risk that, in terms of digital advertising growth rates, Meta could overtake Alphabet by 2026. Thus, it is expected that GOOGL’s advertising revenue will amount to $239.54 billion, whereas META’s will be $243.46 billion. Even though Alphabet has its own levers to increase advertising revenue, it is likely that they will be used only to a limited extent. YouTube, however, is generating billions in other revenue through subscriptions while sacrificing advertising revenue. Thus, this expected advantage for Meta can be viewed not as a consequence of GOOGL’s problems, but rather as a consequence of the latter’s broader revenue diversification.

GOOGL’s Partnership with Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL)

One of the main recent developments surrounding GOOGL involves its partnership agreement with AVGO and MRVL, custom chip manufacturers. In a previous analysis a month ago, where I evaluated these companies side by side, Marvell emerged as the more attractive option in my view. The reason for this is both Marvell’s narrower specialization and its more attractive market multiples. Given that Alphabet is a leader among hyperscalers, finding the most effective solutions for using TPUs is crucial for the company. Within the framework of defining the partners’ roles, it appears that AVGO is the more key ally for GOOGL. Their custom chip deliveries increase the efficiency of Alphabet’s computing power by 2–3 times. That’s super important specifically for training Gemini models. If they buy Nvidia (NVDA) chips like the H100/B200 instead, it brings down infrastructure TCO, and that’s a big deal for the efficiency of CapEx investments.

The appearance of Marvell in this story demonstrates GOOGL’s intention to diversify its supply of custom chips, shifting away from a high concentration of suppliers. One advantage MRVL may have is that it is a leader in the DSP chip segment, allowing thousands of TPUs to be combined into a single supercomputer. The solution enhances the efficiency of the AI infrastructure. In general, Marvell's appearance among suppliers fosters healthy competition, and should affect the cost of equipment purchases. Furthermore, working with MRVL is specifically aimed at developing proprietary chips. In the future, it will reduce GOOGL's operating expenses, since the objective of development is to lower energy and maintenance costs. Also, keep in mind that these partnerships are backed by direct contracts, and they're usually less affected by spot price volatility.

3 Reasons for My "Buy" Rating

Turning to the assessment of GOOGL’s investment appeal, I have decided to assign the stock a "Buy" rating. If we evaluate the company’s strategy and fundamental changes in its business, there are 3 conceptual reasons why these shares are worth buying.

  • Firstly, the company is successfully transitioning to an agent-based cloud, making it more than just a rental service—it is a full-fledged operating system for various AI agents. An example of this is the recent launch of the Gemini Enterprise Agent platform, designed to automate business processes by performing operational tasks within enterprises.
  • Secondly, thanks to its collaboration with AVGO and MRVL, GOOGL is building a diversified supply chain. It involves partners in the development of custom chips for specific tasks (first and foremost, AI models in the cloud). It is the only hyperscaler that isn’t just waiting in line for NVDA chips but is setting the rules for designers to build a fully independent AI infrastructure.
  • Finally, by justifying its enormous CapEx, it creates an investment moat that acts as a barrier to entry for other players. Spending on AI infrastructure is so massive that it effectively imposes a "leadership tax" on competitors. This means that GOOGL is building a physical barrier of record-breaking computing power, setting itself apart as the leader in the world of hyperscalable AI. When you are undoubtedly number one, you can dictate your rules even more forcefully to others, including partners and suppliers.

GOOGL Valuation

Assessing the potential growth of GOOGL shares, one should take into account that the P/E ratio stands at 31.42x, higher than the 10-year average of 27.6x. In one sense, the valuation is overpriced, particularly as analysts expect EPS to decline in Q1 2026. One major stumbling block is the ambitious CapEx plan of up to $185 billion, doubling the company's CapEx compared to 2025. That said, because the company's CapEx is so high, valuing it using the traditional P/E ratio would not be objective.

My view is that CapEx efficiency will exceed market expectations for two reasons:

  • Firstly, the scale-up in capital expenditures aligns with growing demand for cloud services. This is evidenced by Google Cloud’s $240 billion backlog.
  • Secondly, Google is opting for vertical integration and partnerships with AVGO and MRVL, enabling it to create its own AI chips. The result will be lower costs per unit of computing power in the future. It is precisely this factor that will allow the company to maintain its current margins.

In spite of the temporary pressure that CapEx is putting on earnings and FCF, it seems to me that the company has the potential for market value growth. Looking at the forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples, we see 8.55x and 18.47x, respectively. Based on the assumption that GOOGL will trade at multiples of 10x and 20x, the stock price could rise by 17% and 8%. Median growth would be 12.5%. The assessment is conservative because it doesn't factor in the potential for accelerated monetization of AI tools in Google Cloud. For a more optimistic scenario, the stock could reach 12x and 24x multiples, which would boost market value by 35% by 2027.

CapEx Risks in AI Investments

One of the main risks associated with investing in GOOGL stock is the continued aggressive burning of capital amid record-high CapEx, which may not live up to initial expectations in terms of economic viability. Without a doubt, enormous capital investments are critically important, since without them it would be impossible to scale cloud infrastructure. With aggressive competition currently underway among hyperscalers for new markets (often with hyperbolic sales growth), Alphabet is simply forced to increase its CapEx. As the CapEx budget forecast for 2026, which amounts to $185 billion, frightened experts, EPS estimates have been lowered to $2.62. The risk even exists that this could cause FCF to turn negative for the first time in the last decade.

Moreover, the large-scale financing of AI infrastructure has led the company to integrate it into all its products and business processes. As an example, its AI assistant has been integrated into search queries, which has resulted in zero-click queries reaching a record 88% in certain segments. While GOOGL maintains its more than 89% share of the global search market, on the other hand, more profitable information and research queries are being captured by other AI platforms. As a result, questions arise regarding how successfully Alphabet monetizes AI responses, given that the cost of such a query is higher than that of a traditional search.

Conclusion

Therefore, GOOGL represents a unique case that combines daunting risks with a strong foundation for building a powerful investment moat. Unlike its competitors, Alphabet is systematically executing a strategy to build a vertically integrated AI ecosystem. This is why, for the past few months, its partnership with AVGO and MRVL to develop proprietary chips for its computing infrastructure has been a key topic of discussion. For other hyperscalers, Alphabet will remain the most dangerous competitor because it is creating a universal operating system for AI agents that captures cash flow from both cloud providers and the software business. But we can't rule out risks related to a drop in EPS, a decrease in FCF, plus an increase in the volume of unfulfilled contracts.

As an investor who loves to pick assets with strong fundamental value, Alphabet is a clear "Buy" candidate in my view. And I’d be interested to hear your thoughts on the question: Will the explosive growth of GOOGL’s cloud segment outweigh the risks of a temporary decline in earnings and free cash flow? Please feel free to share your thoughts in the comments. You may just inspire me with a new idea for my next article.
